content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

April 5, 1976

Mr. Glenn Durfee
County Counsel
County of Sierra
Courthouse
Downieville, CA 95936

Dear Mr. Durfee:

Re: Request for Opinion
No. 76-036

Thank you for your letter dated March 17, 1976, request-
ing the Fair Political Practices Commission to issue an
opinion concerning the possible conflict of interest created
by a newspaper editor holding office as a county supervisor.
For the reasons stated herein, the Commission will not issue
a formal opinion. However, I hope my informal comments will
be helpful to you in dealing with the situation you present.
No formal opinion will be issued in this matter because the
opinion request fails to identify the person whose duties
are in question and fails to indicate whether you are repre-
senting that person. See 2 Cal. Adm. Code section 18320(a).

In responding to your inquiry, the following considera-
tions appear relevant. Initially, your question asks whether
it can be an unlawful conflict of interest for a certain
person to hold a certain office. The simple answer to that
is that the Political Reform Act does not preclude anyone
from holding any office; rather, the Act creates certain
restrictions on the actions of certain officeholders. Gov.
Code Sections 87100 and 87103 provide:

"No public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or in any
way attempt to use his official position to influence a
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason
to know he has a financial interest."

Section 87100.

"An official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on
the public generally, on:

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE/ADMINISTRATION (916) 322-5660 • EXECUTIVE/LEGAL 322 3901 • ENFORCEMENT/CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322 6441

Mr. Glenn Durfee
April 5, 1976
Page Two


     (a) Any business entity in which the public official
has a direct or indirect investment worth more than one
thousand dollars ($1,000);

     (b) Any real property in which the public official has
a direct or indirect interest worth more than one thou-
sand dollars ($1,000);

     (c) Any source of income, other than loans by a com-
mercial lending institution in the regular course of
business, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250)
or more in value received by or promised to the public
official within twelve months prior to the time when
the decision is made; or

     (d) Any business entity in which the public official
is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or
holds any position of management.

     For purposes of this section, indirect investment or
interest means any investment or interest owned by the
spouse or dependent child of a public official, by an
agent on behalf of a public official, by any business
entity controlled by the public official or by a trust
in which he has a substantial interest. A business
entity is controlled by a public official if the public
official, his agents, spouse and dependent children
hold more than fifty percent of the ownership interest
in the entity. A public official has a substantial
interest in a trust when the official, his spouse and
dependent children have a present or future interest
worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000)."

                                        Section 87103.

     From your questions, there appears to be some doubt
whether a decision of the public official would have a "mater-
ial financial effect" upon a financial interest as defined
in Gov. Code Section 87103. The enclosed regulations may
provide some guidance in this respect. Furthermore, even if
a decision of the public official would have a material
financial effect on a financial interest, the requirements
of the Political Reform Act would be met as long as the
Supervisor did not make or participate in making the de-
cision or in any way use his official position to influence
the decision.

Mr. Glenn Durfee
April 5, 1976
Page Three


Finally, I would feel remiss if I did not mention the
existence of conflict of interest legislation concerning the
making of contracts found in Gov. Code Section 1090, et seq.
Without commenting on the applicability of those provisions
to the facts cited in your letter, I would direct your atten-
tion to those provisions.

If you wish to appeal the denial of your opinion request,
you may do so pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18321.

Thank you for your interest in the Political Reform
Act. If you need further assistance in this matter, please
call Kenneth Goshorn of the Commission's Conflict of Interest
Division, (916) 322-6441.

Sincerely,

[signature]
Daniel H. Lowenstein
Chairman

DHL:pvp

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a newspaper editor holding office as a county supervisor creates an unlawful conflict of interest under California's Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC declines to issue a formal opinion because the request failed to identify the specific person whose duties are in question and failed to indicate whether the requester represents that person. However, informally, the Commission notes that the Political Reform Act does not preclude anyone from holding any office, but rather restricts the actions of officeholders regarding decisions in which they have financial interests as defined by Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Declination letter with informal guidance provided. Request No. 76-036 dated March 17, 1976. Document contains quotations of Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103 defining financial interests. No formal QUESTION and CONCLUSION sections present; structured as letter with informal analysis.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether a newspaper editor holding office as a county supervisor creates an unlawful conflict of interest under California's Political Reform Act.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC declines to issue a formal opinion because the request failed to identify the specific person whose duties are in question and failed to indicate whether the requester represents that person. However, informally, the Commission notes that the Political Reform Act does not preclude anyone from holding any office, but rather restricts the actions of officeholders regarding decisions in which they have financial interests as defined by Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103.

embedding.summary: This is a declination letter in which the FPPC refuses to issue a formal opinion on whether a newspaper editor serving as a county supervisor creates a conflict of interest, citing insufficient information in the request. The FPPC provides informal guidance explaining that the Political Reform Act restricts officeholder conduct regarding decisions affecting their financial interests rather than prohibiting them from holding office.
